skill.name: git-advanced-workflows
skill.description: Guides advanced Git workflows for rebasing, cherry-picking, bisecting, worktrees, reflog recovery, and safer history cleanup.
skill.summary: Use advanced Git workflows to clean history, backport fixes, debug regressions, and recover lost work.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. AI review found these are Markdown Git examples and documentation text, with no executable code, network access, credential handling, or prompt injection. The skill remains medium risk because users may run history-rewriting or destructive Git commands from the guide.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Workflow Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents legitimate Git operations that can rewrite history, force-push branches, or discard local changes. These are useful workflows, but users should verify branch ownership and backups before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands include force-with-lease and reset --hard examples that can alter repository history or discard changes. The surrounding text presents them as Git recovery and cleanup guidance, so this is legitimate but operationally risky.

medium_findings.title: Local Script Execution During Bisect
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends automated bisect commands that run local test scripts or package scripts. This is standard Git usage, but executing repository scripts can run untrusted code in unsafe repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly invoke ./test.sh and npm test through git bisect run. This is not malicious in context, but it is a real execution risk when applied to untrusted repositories.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many external command patterns, but the evidence is Markdown inline code and fenced bash examples. No executable Ruby, shell wrapper, or automation script is present in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns appear inside SKILL.md documentation blocks rather than executable source files. The content is consistent with a Git training guide and lacks user input handling or command construction.

low_findings.title: Relative Worktree Paths Are Not Path Traversal Code
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are relative paths used in Git worktree examples. They do not read, write, or delete arbitrary filesystem paths through skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../ paths are arguments to documented git worktree and cd examples. There is no code that accepts external paths or performs filesystem traversal automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to a front matter description and a sentence about descriptive commit messages. No cryptographic algorithm or security primitive appears at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found ordinary Git documentation text, not MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic usage. This is a scanner misclassification.

dangerous_patterns.title: History-Rewriting and Hard Reset Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include rebase, force-with-lease, and reset --hard. These are appropriate for advanced Git workflows, but they can cause data loss or collaboration conflicts if misapplied.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and have known destructive effects in Git. The skill also includes safeguards, so the concern is operational misuse rather than malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Script Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use git bisect run with local scripts and package tests. This can execute repository-controlled commands when users apply the workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and standard for automated bisecting. Risk depends on repository trust and user execution context.

content.value_statement: Complex Git history can slow reviews and make recovery stressful. This skill gives practical workflows for rebase, cherry-pick, bisect, worktrees, and reflog.
actual_capabilities: Guides interactive rebase cleanup, including squashing, rewording, reordering, and dropping commits.
Shows cherry-pick workflows for single commits, commit ranges, staged picks, and release backports.
Explains manual and automated Git bisect workflows for finding bug-introducing commits.
Describes Git worktree setup and cleanup for parallel branch development.
Provides reflog recovery patterns for lost commits, deleted branches, and bad resets.
Covers safer force pushing, backup branches, and common Git workflow pitfalls.
limitations: It does not execute Git commands or inspect a repository by itself.
Examples use placeholder commits, branch names, and paths that users must adapt.
History rewriting requires coordination when commits are shared with other people.
Recovery guidance depends on reflog availability and local repository state.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Clean Pull Request
use_cases.description: Use rebase, autosquash, and safer force push practices to present a readable branch history before review.

use_cases.title: Backport a Hotfix
use_cases.description: Apply one fix across release branches with cherry-pick workflows and clear conflict recovery steps.

use_cases.title: Find a Regression
use_cases.description: Use manual or automated bisect steps to locate the commit that introduced a bug.

prompt_templates.title: Clean My Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner cleanup before opening a pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me clean up my Git branch before a pull request. Explain which rebase steps to use and what to check before pushing.

prompt_templates.title: Cherry-Pick a Fix
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through cherry-picking one commit into two release branches. Include conflict handling and safe abort steps.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Bisect Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Git bisect plan for a regression. Include how to choose good and bad commits, test each step, and reset afterward.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Recovery Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced recovery after a risky history operation.
prompt_templates.prompt: I ran a risky Git history command and may have lost work. Build a recovery plan using reflog, backup branches, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to tidy five messy commits before review.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a rebase-focused checklist: confirm branch ownership, create a backup branch, squash fixups, reword messages, run tests, and push with lease.

output_examples.input: A maintainer needs to identify when a regression began.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines a bisect session with good and bad commit selection, repeated test decisions, final commit confirmation, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: A release engineer must apply one security fix to older releases.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests cherry-picking the fix into each release branch, resolving conflicts separately, and validating each branch before publishing.

best_practices: Create a backup branch before complex rebases, hard resets, or recovery experiments.
Use force-with-lease instead of force when updating a rewritten personal branch.
Run tests and review history before pushing changes created by advanced Git workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not rebase public branches without coordinating with collaborators.
Do not run automated bisect scripts from repositories you do not trust.
Do not use hard reset until you understand which changes will be discarded.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples that users must review and run themselves.

faq.question: Is interactive rebase safe for shared branches?
faq.answer: It can disrupt collaborators. Use it mainly on local or personal branches unless the team agrees.

faq.question: When should I use cherry-pick instead of merge?
faq.answer: Use cherry-pick when you need specific commits without bringing an entire branch history.

faq.question: What does git bisect help with?
faq.answer: It narrows a regression to the commit that introduced the problem by testing selected revisions.

faq.question: Can reflog recover every lost commit?
faq.answer: No. Recovery depends on local reflog retention and whether Git has pruned unreachable objects.

faq.question: Why is this skill medium risk?
faq.answer: It teaches legitimate commands that can rewrite history, discard changes, or run local scripts if users apply them.
